content.decision_question: Should transparent minimum-viability thresholds triggering default closure (with compensatory mitigation) be adopted, or should policy prioritize preventing closure through networked/hub retention models even where per-village viability is marginal?
content.evidence_boundary: Further evidence (a Hungary-specific outcome registry) could clarify whether closures in Hungary actually accelerate depopulation as in Finland/Denmark or show no effect as in Saxony/Sweden, and could verify whether consolidation produces real net fiscal savings once transport costs are counted. It cannot resolve the prior value question of how much weight to give community/social-capital loss relative to fiscal efficiency, nor can it eliminate the equity concern that closure disproportionately affects Roma and low-mobility families, which is a distributive value judgment, not merely an empirical one.
content.tension: T5 accepts closure as the default where enrollment thresholds are unmet, compensating via transport, funding transparency, and equity support, while T4 attempts to avoid closure altogether through networked clustering and hub conversion. The tension is whether individual-level mitigation (transport, funding) can substitute for the community-level and social-capital losses (documented in Polish and Danish/Finnish evidence) that closure produces, especially given contested evidence on whether closure itself accelerates depopulation.
content.title: Default closure with mitigation versus community/network preservation
value_poles: administrative transparency and predictable fiscal management
preservation of community/social capital and local cohesion
equity protection for the most vulnerable (Roma, low-income) families
